_**Agent (premium)**_

Implemented the full source-of-truth experience from `PRODUCT_SPEC.md` using the `frontend-design` + `video-to-website` workflows, and rebuilt the project outputs.

I used the local `commercial.mp4` in the repo because `/Users/st/Downloads/commercial.mp4` was not present on disk.

### Updated Files
- `index.html`
- `styles.css`
- `script.js`
- `frames/frame_0001.jpg` through `frames/frame_0146.jpg` (regenerated at 12 fps)
- `build/index.html`
- `build/styles.css`
- `build/script.js`
- `build/frames/*` (via `npm run build` copy step)

### Visual Direction
The page now follows a clean, Apple-inspired, white-canvas storytelling direction with large typography, minimal UI chrome, precise scroll choreography, and a premium fintech tone. Content is progressively revealed over a pinned cinematic sequence, with distinct section rhythms (left/right alternation, marquee, stats, product clarity, persistent CTA) to avoid repetitive layout patterns and keep motion intentional.

### Key Implementation Settings
- Frame source: `commercial.mp4` (repo file fallback)
- Video metadata: `1792x1024`, `12.2s`, `30fps`, `366` source frames
- Extracted frames: `146` JPG frames (`fps=12`)
- Frame progression speed: `FRAME_SPEED = 2.05` (accelerated so key motion resolves earlier)
- Critical preload: first `10` frames, then background load
- Scroll length: `920vh` (`STORY_SCROLL = 9200`)
- Canvas draw mode: padded cover with `IMAGE_SCALE = 0.88` + DPR scaling
- Motion stack: Lenis + GSAP ScrollTrigger
- Timeline content aligned to spec:
  - Segment B: Charles Barkley
  - Segment C: Richard Fairbank
  - Segment D: Trusted by millions
  - Segment E: SavorOne / Platinum / Venture / QuickSilver
- Final CTA persistence: enabled with `data-persist="true"`

### Validation Checklist
- Smooth scrolling behavior: implemented (Lenis + ScrollTrigger sync)
- Frame rendering responsiveness: implemented (pinned canvas + accelerated mapping)
- Distinct adjacent section animations: implemented (`fade-up`, `slide-left`, `rotate-in`, `stagger-up`, `clip-reveal`, `scale-up`)
- Marquee + counters: implemented (scrubbed marquee + count-up stats)
- Overlay and hero reveal timing: implemented (dark overlay pulse + circle clip reveal)
- Final CTA persistence: implemented and non-reversing
- Lint status on edited files: no linter errors reported

If you want, I can now tune pacing pass-by-pass (for example: slower hero hold, later card reveal, or stronger stats overlay window) after one visual review run.

_**User**_

how to install libwebp with ffmpeg?

---

_**Agent (premium)**_

On macOS (Homebrew), quickest path is:

```bash
brew install webp
brew reinstall ffmpeg --build-from-source
```

Then verify WebP support:

```bash
ffmpeg -hide_banner -encoders | rg webp
ffmpeg -hide_banner -buildconf | rg webp
```

You should see `--enable-libwebp` and at least one WebP encoder (`libwebp` / `libwebp_anim`).

### If it still doesn’t appear
Homebrew’s preconfigured formula may still miss it depending on your local setup. In that case, build FFmpeg manually with explicit flag:

```bash
brew install pkg-config webp
git clone https://git.ffmpeg.org/ffmpeg.git ffmpeg-src
cd ffmpeg-src
./configure --enable-gpl --enable-version3 --enable-libwebp
make -j"$(sysctl -n hw.ncpu)"
sudo make install
```

If you want, I can give you a **safe side-by-side install** command set (so it doesn’t overwrite your current `/usr/local/bin/ffmpeg`).

---

_**User**_

ffmpeg -hide_banner -encoders | rg webp
ffmpeg -hide_banner -buildconf | rg webp

nothing pops up

---

_**Agent (premium)**_

Exactly — if both commands return nothing, your current `ffmpeg` binary was compiled **without WebP support**.

### Key point
You can’t “add” `libwebp` to an existing `ffmpeg` install.  
You need a **new ffmpeg build** compiled with `--enable-libwebp`.

## Fastest practical options (macOS)

### Option A: Build ffmpeg from source with libwebp
```bash
brew install pkg-config nasm yasm libwebp x264 x265 libvpx opus lame dav1d

cd /tmp
curl -LO https://ffmpeg.org/releases/ffmpeg-8.1.tar.xz
tar -xf ffmpeg-8.1.tar.xz
cd ffmpeg-8.1

./configure \
  --prefix=/usr/local/opt/ffmpeg-webp \
  --enable-gpl \
  --enable-version3 \
  --enable-openssl \
  --enable-libwebp \
  --enable-libx264 \
  --enable-libx265 \
  --enable-libvpx \
  --enable-libopus \
  --enable-libmp3lame \
  --enable-libdav1d

make -j"$(sysctl -n hw.ncpu)"
make install
```

Then use that binary explicitly:
```bash
/usr/local/opt/ffmpeg-webp/bin/ffmpeg -hide_banner -encoders | rg webp
/usr/local/opt/ffmpeg-webp/bin/ffmpeg -hide_banner -buildconf | rg libwebp
```

### Option B: Use a prebuilt ffmpeg that already includes libwebp
If you use another distributor (not your current Homebrew formula), verify first:
```bash
ffmpeg -hide_banner -encoders | rg webp
```

---

If you want, I can give you a **copy-paste one-liner** to regenerate your frames as `.webp` immediately once the new ffmpeg is installed.

---

_**User**_

how to reinstall ffmpeg with --enable-libwebp

---

_**Agent (premium)**_

Yep — if both checks return nothing, your current FFmpeg truly has **no WebP encoder support**.

### Fastest fix (Homebrew)
On macOS, don’t pass `--enable-libwebp` manually to `brew install`; Homebrew formula handles flags internally. Reinstall from clean Homebrew core and make sure the right binary is used:

```bash
brew update
brew uninstall ffmpeg
brew install ffmpeg
hash -r
type -a ffmpeg
ffmpeg -hide_banner -encoders | rg -i webp
ffmpeg -hide_banner -buildconf | rg -i webp
```

You should see encoders like `libwebp` / `libwebp_anim`.

---

### If Homebrew still gives no WebP
You likely have a conflicting/custom FFmpeg binary. Check:

```bash
type -a ffmpeg
which ffmpeg
ls -l "$(which ffmpeg)"
```

If it’s not the Homebrew one you expect, fix PATH or unlink/remove that binary.

---

### Guaranteed method: build FFmpeg yourself with `--enable-libwebp`
```bash
brew install webp pkg-config nasm yasm x264 x265 libvpx

curl -LO https://ffmpeg.org/releases/ffmpeg-8.1.tar.xz
tar -xf ffmpeg-8.1.tar.xz
cd ffmpeg-8.1

./configure \
  --prefix="$HOME/.local/ffmpeg-webp" \
  --enable-gpl \
  --enable-libx264 \
  --enable-libx265 \
  --enable-libvpx \
  --enable-libwebp

make -j"$(sysctl -n hw.ncpu)"
make install
```

Then add to PATH (zsh):
```bash
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/ffmpeg-webp/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.zshrc
source ~/.zshrc
```

Verify:
```bash
ffmpeg -hide_banner -encoders | rg -i webp
ffmpeg -hide_banner -buildconf | rg -i webp
```

If you want, I can give you a copy-paste block tailored to your exact Homebrew prefix (`/usr/local` vs `/opt/homebrew`).

_**Agent (premium)**_

Re-ran the implementation from `PRODUCT_SPEC.md` with `commercial.mp4` and converted the frame pipeline to WebP, while preserving the Apple-style scroll choreography.

### Generated/Updated File Paths
- `frames/frame_0001.webp` … `frames/frame_0146.webp` (new WebP sequence)
- `script.js` (frame loader now points to `.webp`)
- `build/` assets regenerated via `npm run build` (includes updated `build/script.js` + copied `frames/`)
- `package.json` and `package-lock.json` (added `sharp` for local JPG→WebP conversion fallback)

### Chosen Visual Direction
The experience remains a minimal, white-canvas, Apple-inspired fintech narrative: oversized typographic hierarchy, restrained UI chrome, progressive callout reveals tied to scroll phases, and a clean transition from cinematic motion to product clarity with the four card variants and persistent final CTA.

### Key Implementation Settings
- Source video: `commercial.mp4`
- Video analysis: `1792x1024`, `12.2s`, `30fps`
- Extracted sequence: `146` frames at `12fps`
- Final frame format: **WebP only** in `frames/`
- Scroll pacing: `STORY_SCROLL = 9200` (`~920vh`)
- Frame mapping: `FRAME_SPEED = 2.05`
- Canvas scaling: `IMAGE_SCALE = 0.88`
- Loader strategy: first `10` frames critical preload, then background loading

### GSAP / ScrollTrigger Double-Check
- `gsap.registerPlugin(ScrollTrigger)` is correctly initialized.
- Lenis sync is correct:
  - `lenis.on("scroll", ScrollTrigger.update)`
  - `gsap.ticker.add(...)`
  - `gsap.ticker.lagSmoothing(0)`
- Main pinned narrative is correctly scrubbed via a single `ScrollTrigger` on `.story-wrap`.
- Callouts are scroll-windowed and eased in/out by segment ranges.
- Section choreography follows label → heading → body → CTA sequencing.
- Marquee and dark-overlay are independently scrubbed and synchronized.
- Final CTA persistence via `data-persist="true"` is intact.

### Important Toolchain Note
Your installed `ffmpeg` lacks `libwebp`/`webp` encoders, so I used a reliable fallback:
1. extract temporary JPG frames with ffmpeg,
2. convert to WebP using `sharp`,
3. remove temporary JPGs,
4. keep only `.webp` in `frames/`.

If you want, I can next remove any now-unused JPG artifacts from `build/frames` and run one more cleanup pass for a fully WebP-only repo state.

_**Agent (premium)**_

Fixed likely blocker in `script.js`:

- Removed `pin` behavior from the main `ScrollTrigger`
- Switched progress tracking to `.story-scroll` directly (`start: "top top"`, `end: "bottom bottom"`)
- Kept `.canvas-shell` as fixed via CSS only (cleaner, more reliable)
- Cleaned the unused `STORY_SCROLL` constant

This avoids the fragile pattern of pinning an already fixed element, which can cause frame progression not to update.

### Please test now
- Serve root: `python3 -m http.server 4173`
- Open: `http://localhost:4173/index.html`
- Hard refresh once (`Cmd+Shift+R`)
- Scroll past hero into story

If it still doesn’t animate, next step is I add a temporary debug readout in the corner (`progress`, `currentFrame`, `loadedCount`) so we can confirm whether ScrollTrigger or image decode is the remaining issue in your environment.

_**User**_

Use the /video-to-website and /frontend-design 

the frames are already generated in @frames so can skip the ffprobe and ffmpeg steps

Build a one-page landing page using only:
- vanilla HTML
- vanilla CSS
- vanilla JavaScript
- GSAP with ScrollTrigger

Do not use React, Next.js, Tailwind, or any framework.

Use the design/content requirements below, but do NOT use normal video playback for the main scroll sequence.

Important:
- The main animation must use the image frames in `/frames`
- Render the frame sequence with a single HTML canvas
- Map scroll progress to the frame index with GSAP ScrollTrigger
- Do not swap img tags for every frame
- Use canvas rendering for smooth performance
- The page should feel premium, modern, minimal, and professional
- White background throughout
- Dark readable text
- Smooth Apple-style scroll storytelling

Build the page with these sections:
1. Hero
2. Pinned scroll animation section
3. Card highlights section
4. Final CTA section

Hero section content:
- Eyebrow: "Premium credit experiences"
- Headline: "A smarter way to experience credit."
- Supporting paragraph: "Discover a modern portfolio of credit cards built around flexibility, rewards, and confidence for everyday life."
- Scroll hint: "Scroll to explore"

Pinned scroll animation section requirements:
- Use the frames in `/frames` as the source for the animation
- Preload the frames
- Draw them into a canvas
- Pin this section during scroll
- Sync frame progression to scroll progress using GSAP ScrollTrigger
- The animation should feel smooth and cinematic, not like a normal video scrub
- The background should stay white and blend seamlessly with the page
- Add floating callout cards that fade in and out at specific scroll ranges
- Keep callouts positioned so they do not cover the main visual

Callout content and timing:
1. Charles Barkley moment
   - appears during the basketball-player portion of the animation
   - label: "Charles Barkley"
   - paragraph: "Charles Barkley is one of basketball’s most recognizable figures, known for his Hall of Fame career, larger-than-life presence, and decades of influence across sports and media."

2. Richard Fairbank moment
   - appears during the executive portion of the animation
   - label: "Richard Fairbank"
   - paragraph: "Richard Fairbank is the longtime founder and leader of Capital One, widely recognized for helping shape the company into one of the most prominent names in consumer finance."

3. Capital One cards moment
   - appears during the credit card portion of the animation
   - label: "Trusted by millions"
   - paragraph: "Capital One credit cards are recognized for combining everyday usability with strong rewards, flexible options, and a trusted brand experience designed for modern consumers."

Callout animation behavior:
- opacity: 0 -> 1 -> 0
- slight translateY or translateX movement
- smooth premium easing
- subtle floating feel
- readable white/near-white cards with dark text
- rounded corners and soft border/shadow

Suggested scroll ranges for callouts:
- Charles Barkley: 15%–35%
- Richard Fairbank: 35%–55%
- Capital One cards: 55%–78%

Card highlights section:
Create a clean premium section after the pinned animation highlighting these four cards:
- SavorOne — "Made for everyday spending with rewarding value across the moments that matter most."
- Platinum — "A straightforward credit option designed for simplicity, confidence, and dependable everyday use."
- Venture — "Built for people who value travel, flexibility, and rewards that go further."
- QuickSilver — "Simple unlimited cash back with a clean, easy-to-understand experience."

Final CTA section:
- Headline: "Choose a card built for how you live."
- Body: "From everyday simplicity to travel-focused rewards, explore credit options designed to support your goals with confidence."
- Primary CTA: "Explore cards"
- Secondary CTA: "Learn more"

HTML structure guidance:
- header
- main
  - section.hero
  - section.scroll-story
    - pinned wrapper
    - canvas container
    - floating callouts container
  - section.card-highlights
  - section.cta
- footer

Create production-ready files:
- index.html
- styles.css
- script.js

Implementation notes:
- Use semantic HTML
- Add clear comments in script.js for:
  - frame preloading
  - canvas rendering
  - ScrollTrigger setup
  - callout timing
- Keep motion smooth and refined
- Use transforms and opacity where possible
- Make the final result feel polished, launch-ready, and high-end

---

Implemented the full canvas-based scroll story using your already-generated `frames/` (no ffmpeg/ffprobe step), with vanilla HTML/CSS/JS + GSAP/ScrollTrigger only.

### Generated/updated files
- `index.html`
- `styles.css`
- `script.js`

### What this now does
- Uses a **single `canvas`** (`#story-canvas`) for the main animation
- Preloads frame images from `frames/frame_0001.webp` ... `frame_0146.webp`
- Pins the scroll story section and maps scroll progress to frame index
- Uses smooth acceleration (`FRAME_SPEED = 2.0`) for responsive cinematic pacing
- Adds three floating callouts with requested copy and timing windows:
  - Charles Barkley (`15%–35%`)
  - Richard Fairbank (`35%–55%`)
  - Trusted by millions (`55%–78%`)
- Keeps design premium/minimal:
  - white background throughout
  - dark readable text
  - subtle card styling, rounded corners, soft shadows
- Includes required sections:
  1. Hero
  2. Pinned scroll animation section
  3. Card highlights
  4. Final CTA
- Adds semantic footer

### Notes on JS comments
`script.js` includes clear comments for:
- frame preloading
- canvas rendering
- ScrollTrigger setup
- callout timing

If you want, I can do one final timing pass to align each callout to exact visual beats in your `commercial.mp4` sequence (by tuning the `data-start/data-end` ranges).
